text: The minimally invasive spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy has become a routine procedure for management of benign or low-grade tumours of the body and tail of the pancreas.
The surgeon demonstrates the temporary occlusion of the splenic artery using robotic instruments during a spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy.
This modification of the Kimura technique was developed at the Royal North Shore Hospital in Sydney, and is successfully being used for our last twelve robotic distal pancreatectomies.
The surgeon applies a vascular clamp to the splenic artery, temporarily occluding it to reduce arterial inflow to the spleen and indirectly decrease venous return.
The surgical field remains relatively bloodless, facilitating the precise dissection of the non-distended splenic vein.
The da Vinci robotic platform is utilized to perform the spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy, showcasing the robotic instruments maneuvering within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting the gastrocolic ligament along the greater curvature of the stomach, providing access to the lesser sac.
The surgeon uses sutures to hitch the stomach onto the abdominal wall, providing retraction and improved access to the surgical field.
The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to scan the surface of the pancreas, identifying the precise location of the tumor and its proximity to the portal vein and the neck of the pancreas.
Using the da Vinci robotic platform, the surgeon begins dissection along the inferior border of the pancreas, employing a medial to lateral approach to carefully separate the tissue.
The surgeon identifies the splenic vein and carefully dissects and divides the venous tributaries connecting it to the pancreas using the da Vinci robotic platform.
The surgeon encounters a tear in a venous tributary during dissection and applies a surgical clip to control the bleeding.
The surgeon observes rapid blood loss from an extended tear in a venous tributary, highlighting the absence of proximal inflow control during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a 6-0 PDS suture to successfully tamponade the bleeding vein, effectively controlling the blood loss from the venous tear.
The surgeon prepares to divide the neck of the pancreas lateral to the portal vein, positioning instruments to ensure precise separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon prepares to divide the neck of the pancreas, choosing between using a stapler or diathermy for the procedure.
In a patient with a bulky or firm pancreas, the experience at this centre has been that the stapler does not efficiently span or cut across the neck.
The surgeon uses a diathermy device to divide the neck of the pancreas, as the stapler was not suitable for this case.
After dividing the pancreatic parenchyma, the surgeon identifies the splenic artery running along the superior margin of the pancreas.
The surgeon clips and divides the pancreatic magna branch, which is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon gently mobilizes the splenic artery away from the pancreatic parenchyma, ensuring careful separation of the artery from the surrounding tissue.
A vascular clamp is introduced through a 12mm port into the abdominal cavity and applied across the splenic artery, securing the vessel for further manipulation.
Vessel loops are placed across the splenic artery and vein, ready to be tightened in case of inadvertent bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon employs the Royal North Shore technique to achieve immediate inflow occlusion, facilitating the dissection towards the splenic hilum with minimal blood loss.
A clamp is applied, freeing an additional robotic arm for suture ligation at sites of bleeding within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the splenic hilum and encounters bleeding from a small venous tributary, requiring careful management to control the bleeding.
The surgeon observes low flow bleeding from a venous tributary near the splenic hilum, which is visibly reduced due to the application of a vascular clamp across the splenic artery.
The surgical field is kept clean with minimal blood loss, allowing the surgeon to identify the defect for suture ligation using a 6-0 PDS suture.
The surgeon has cleared the tail of the pancreas from the splenic hilum, successfully dividing all venous tributaries and arterial branches. The focus now shifts to controlling the proximal stump.

The surgeon uses a 4-0 Prolene suture to control the proximal main pancreatic duct, followed by a continuous V-lock suture to secure the pancreatic resection margin, ensuring the stability of the surgical site.
The surgeon applies a haemostatic agent to the pancreatic resection bed, compacting it with a small amount of fibrillar material to control bleeding and stabilize the area.
The surgeon removes the vascular clamp, and the omentum is placed over the pancreatic resection bed to cover the area.
The surgical specimen is retrieved through the umbilical port before the closure of the fascia.
We do not routinely use any drains in our institution for these cases.
